stem: Let \( f(z) = ([[c2]])z^{2} + ([[c1]])z + ([[c0]]) \), and let \( C \) be a positively oriented simple closed contour enclosing the point \( z = [[a]] \). By Cauchy's integral formula, evaluate \( \displaystyle\oint_{C} \frac{f(z)}{z - ([[a]])}\,dz \).
solution: Cauchy's integral formula gives \( \oint_{C} \frac{f(z)}{z-a}\,dz = 2\pi i\, f(a) \). Here \( f([[a]]) = ([[c2]])([[a]])^{2} + ([[c1]])([[a]]) + ([[c0]]) \), so the integral equals \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( f(a) \) alone, forgetting the factor \( 2\pi i \).
This uses \( \pi i \) instead of \( 2\pi i \) as the constant.
This has the wrong sign (the contour orientation was reversed).
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by \( 2\pi i \) from the correct value.
This is off by \( 2\pi i \) from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(z) = ([[c3]])z^{3} + ([[c2]])z^{2} + ([[c1]])z + ([[c0]]) \), and let \( C \) be a positively oriented simple closed contour enclosing the point \( z = [[a]] \). By the derivative form of Cauchy's integral formula, evaluate \( \displaystyle\oint_{C} \frac{f(z)}{(z - ([[a]]))^{2}}\,dz \).
solution: The derivative form of Cauchy's integral formula gives \( \oint_{C} \frac{f(z)}{(z-a)^{2}}\,dz = 2\pi i\, f'(a) \). Here \( f'(z) = 3([[c3]])z^{2} + 2([[c2]])z + ([[c1]]) \), so \( f'([[a]]) \) times \( 2\pi i \) equals \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This halves the correct coefficient, as if the \( n=1 \) case needed a \( 1/1! \) applied twice.
This uses \( \pi i \) instead of \( 2\pi i \).
This is \( f'(a) \) alone, forgetting the factor \( 2\pi i \).
This has the wrong sign (the contour orientation was reversed).
This is off by \( 2\pi i \) from the correct value.
This is off by \( 2\pi i \) from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(z) = ([[c3]])z^{3} + ([[c2]])z^{2} + ([[c1]])z + ([[c0]]) \), and let \( C \) be a positively oriented simple closed contour enclosing the point \( z = [[a]] \). By the generalized Cauchy integral formula \( \displaystyle\oint_{C} \frac{f(z)}{(z-a)^{n+1}}\,dz = \frac{2\pi i}{n!}f^{(n)}(a) \), evaluate \( \displaystyle\oint_{C} \frac{f(z)}{(z - ([[a]]))^{3}}\,dz \).
solution: With \( n=2 \), the formula gives \( \oint_{C} \frac{f(z)}{(z-a)^{3}}\,dz = \frac{2\pi i}{2!}f''(a) = \pi i\, f''(a) \). Here \( f''(z) = 6([[c3]])z + 2([[c2]]) \), so \( \pi i\,f''([[a]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by \( 2! = 2 \), using the \( n=1 \) prefactor \( 2\pi i \) instead of \( \pi i \).
This divides by an extra factor of 2, effectively using \( n! \) twice.
This is \( f''(a) \) alone, forgetting the prefactor \( \pi i \).
This has the wrong sign (the contour orientation was reversed).
This is off by \( 2\pi i \) from the correct value.
This is off by \( \pi i \) from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(z) = ([[c2]])z^{2} + ([[c1]])z + ([[c0]]) \), and let \( C \) be the positively oriented circle \( |z| = [[R]] \). Since \( z = [[a]] \) lies outside \( C \), evaluate \( \displaystyle\oint_{C} \frac{f(z)}{z - ([[a]])}\,dz \).
solution: Cauchy's integral formula only applies to a singularity ENCLOSED by \( C \). Since \( z=[[a]] \) lies outside \( |z|=[[R]] \), the integrand \( f(z)/(z-a) \) is analytic everywhere inside and on \( C \), so by the Cauchy-Goursat theorem the integral is \( 0 \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly applies Cauchy's integral formula as if \( z=a \) were enclosed by \( C \).
This evaluates \( f(a) \) as if that were the answer, ignoring that no pole is enclosed.
This treats the integral as \( \oint dz/z \), which is not the integrand here.
This has the wrong sign for the same mistaken residue-style computation.
This applies a residue-style factor to the constant term despite there being no enclosed pole.
This applies a residue-style factor to the constant term despite there being no enclosed pole.
